location.formative: Seattle, WA — Central District, Jackson Street corridor
location.career: Seattle, WA — Central District clubs, University of Washington faculty

examples: Jazz by Standifer sessions (KNKX/KPLU archives) — explicit demonstration of swing subdivision mechanics
Central District club performances (1960s-1990s) — swing feel in live small-ensemble context
UW student recitals under Standifer's direction — pedagogical transmission of swing feel
theory_nodes.description: Swing as asymmetric eighth-note subdivision, approximately 2:1 long-short ratio varying by tempo and style. The triplet underpinning (beat divided into three, first two tied) is the theoretical model; the reality is a fluid range of ratios navigated by ear. Standifer's pedagogical contribution: making implicit swing knowledge explicit — articulating the exact placement of the offbeat, the forward momentum of the long note, the propulsion of the short note into the next beat.

genealogy.relationship: complementary perspective — Anderson (degree 3) introduced swing as elastic time felt by the performer. Standifer provides the analytical mechanics: how the feel works, why the ratio shifts, what the body is doing. Anderson is the practitioner; Standifer is the analyst. Together, degrees 3 and 4 complete the swing picture.

examples: Jazz by Standifer session recordings — warm, lyrical trumpet tone demonstrating soft tonguing
Live Central District performances — trumpet leading small ensemble with melodic clarity
UW faculty recitals — concert-level brass technique in academic context
theory_nodes.description: Embouchure: the configuration of lips, jaw, and facial muscles against the mouthpiece that determines tone quality, range, endurance, and expressive capacity. Articulation: tonguing technique (hard vs. soft) that determines note onset character. Standifer's approach prioritized melodic singing quality over pyrotechnic display — lyrical, connected phrasing that made the trumpet sound almost vocal. First brass-specific theory node in S36.

genealogy.relationship: new branch — first brass-specific theory node. Shares the embodied-instrument principle with Anderson's vocal theory (degree 3): both trumpet and voice produce sound through the vibration of the player's own body (lips for trumpet, vocal cords for voice). The body-as-sound-source thread connects degrees 3 and 4.

theory_nodes.concept: Bebop vocabulary (chromatic approach notes, enclosure patterns)
examples: Standifer trumpet solos on Jackson Street era recordings — chromatic approach in live improvisation
Jazz by Standifer bebop demonstrations — enclosure patterns deconstructed for audience
UW performance archives — bebop vocabulary taught as systematic method
theory_nodes.description: Chromatic approach note: a note one half-step above or below a target chord tone, connecting scale tones with chromatic passing motion. Enclosure pattern: target note approached from both above and below in sequence, creating the characteristic zigzag contour of bebop melody. Standifer taught these as explicit, repeatable techniques — demonstrating the pattern, deconstructing it (upper approach, lower approach, target resolution), and rebuilding it over ii-V-I progressions. Chromaticism as curriculum.

genealogy.relationship: chromaticism thread extended — Jones (compositional choice, bass), Frisell (compositional choice, upper register), Anderson (intuitive inflection, voice), Standifer (codified method, melody). Four deployments: two intuitive (Anderson, Frisell), two systematic (Jones, Standifer). Standifer's contribution is making chromaticism teachable as a repeatable technique.

theory_nodes.concept: The jazz educator as bridge between practice and theory
examples: Jazz by Standifer sessions — performance interleaved with explanation (the format itself is the example)
UW jazz history and theory courses — formal curriculum translating oral tradition into academic framework
Standifer's mentorship of UW students — pedagogical chain extending Jackson Street knowledge to post-corridor generations
theory_nodes.description: Translation of implicit musical knowledge into explicit, teachable form. Jazz has historically transmitted through oral tradition (bandstand apprenticeship, listening, sitting in). Standifer added articulation: naming what other musicians felt, explaining why phrases swing, why substitutions work, why voicings create tension. Fifth position on the composition-improvisation spectrum: pedagogy. The educator's output is not performance or composition but understanding — the student's ability to generate their own music because they possess the theoretical framework.

genealogy.relationship: fifth position on composition-improvisation spectrum — Jones (full composition), Horvitz (composed improvisation), Frisell (free improvisation), Anderson (interpretation), Standifer (pedagogy). The educator does not create new material; he transmits existing material with explanatory context. Output is understanding, not artifact.

seattle_connection: Central District (arrived post-WWII). Jackson Street corridor — direct participation as working musician for decades. University of Washington music faculty — jazz history, theory, and performance courses. Hosted 'Jazz by Standifer' performance-education sessions. KNKX/KPLU radio broadcasts. Died in Seattle, January 2007.

theory_genealogy: Swing rhythm and triplet feel — analytical complement to Anderson's elastic swing (degree 3)
Brass articulation and embouchure — first brass-specific node, body-as-sound-source shared with vocal theory
Bebop vocabulary (chromatic approach, enclosures) — chromaticism as codified, teachable method
Jazz educator as bridge — fifth position on composition-improvisation spectrum (pedagogy)
immediate_items: Overton Berry (degree 5) inherits: Central District elder-statesman parallel, formal/informal pedagogy axis — Standifer as professor, Berry as practitioner, both transmitting the same tradition through different mechanisms
The Garfield pipeline: Standifer's educational path (not confirmed Garfield) vs. Jones/Anderson (confirmed Garfield). Track alternative Central District educational paths.
The Teacher pattern established — classify subsequent artists: do they teach, perform, compose, build, or export?
Body-as-sound-source thread (trumpet lips + vocal cords) — track whether subsequent brass or wind players extend this connection
Bebop vocabulary as codified method — track whether subsequent artists treat chromaticism as intuitive or systematic
pattern_items: Composition-improvisation spectrum now has five positions: full composition (Jones), composed improvisation (Horvitz), free improvisation (Frisell), interpretation (Anderson), pedagogy (Standifer). Continue tracking.
Rhythmic spectrum: four positions remain (regular, polymetric, rubato, elastic swing). Standifer reinforces swing from the analytical side but does not add a new position.
Chromaticism thread now has four deployments: bass-line (Jones), upper-register (Frisell), embodied/vocal (Anderson), codified/melodic (Standifer). Two intuitive, two systematic.

Artist-city taxonomy now has five named patterns: Export (Jones), Draw (Frisell/Horvitz), Exile (Anderson mid-career), Stay (Anderson late career), Teacher (Standifer). Classify every subsequent artist.
Artist-as-institution spectrum now has five positions: industry (Jones), community (Horvitz), performer (Frisell), presence (Anderson), educator (Standifer).
Central District geographic thread: 5 degrees, 4 artists directly connected (Jones, Anderson, Standifer + Berry upcoming). Densest geographic cluster in S36.
Jackson Street primary participants: 3 confirmed (Jones, Anderson, Standifer). Track all future Jackson Street-connected artists.
Brass theory branch introduced (first). Track at subsequent brass/wind player degrees.
Pedagogy as knowledge-transmission chain: Standifer → UW students → their students. Track pedagogical lineages when they appear.
Formal/informal pedagogy axis: Standifer (formal — UW, structured sessions) vs. Anderson (informal — presence) vs. Berry (informal — performance). Track the axis.

degree_neighbors.name: Overton Berry
degree_neighbors.relationship: Central District contemporary, parallel elder-statesman, formal/informal pedagogy axis

degree_neighbors.name: Quincy Jones
degree_neighbors.relationship: Central District peer during Jones's Seattle years, Jackson Street corridor, direct personal overlap

degree_neighbors.name: Ernestine Anderson
degree_neighbors.relationship: Central District contemporary, parallel Seattle jazz anchor — Anderson through performance, Standifer through teaching

garfield_thread.degree_0: Quincy Jones — attended late 1940s, class of 1950. Arranger/producer. Left Seattle.
garfield_thread.degree_3: Ernestine Anderson — attended late 1940s, same era as Jones. Vocalist. Left and returned.
garfield_thread.degree_4: Floyd Standifer — Central District post-WWII, educational path not confirmed as Garfield. Multi-instrumentalist and educator. Never left.
